patches.rationale: Directly supported by the project summary and fronting intent describing governed channel reads, approved message preparation, channel boundaries, audit evidence, and denial of raw Slack access.
patches.value: Provide a governed Slack fronting experience that lets users read bounded channel and thread context, prepare channel communications for review, and work within explicit approval, channel, and audit boundaries instead of using raw Slack tools directly.

patches.rationale: The brief explicitly frames the need as denial of raw Slack tool access and replacement with governed capabilities.
patches.value: Teams need Slack access for context gathering and communication drafting, but raw Slack API or MCP access can bypass channel restrictions, approval controls, and auditability. The product addresses this by exposing governed business capabilities instead of unrestricted Slack operations.

patches.rationale: These goals are explicitly grounded in the project summary, fronting intent, and capability evidence.
value: Enable bounded Slack channel and thread reads for declared context without exposing raw workspace tools.
Support preparation of channel messages, incident updates, and announcement requests without sending them directly.
Enforce channel allow-list and unauthorized posting boundaries through the product experience.
Preserve approval gates and audit evidence for communication-related actions.
Present business-facing governed Slack capabilities rather than raw Slack Web API or MCP operations.

items.body: The product shall present business-facing Slack capabilities for bounded channel context, thread inspection, message preparation, incident update preparation, and announcement request flows. It shall not expose raw Slack Web API operations, raw MCP operations, or direct workspace tool access to agents or end users.
items.rationale: The fronting intent explicitly says agents should see governed Slack capabilities, not raw Slack Web API or MCP operations, and calls for explicit denial of raw Slack tool access.
items.title: Expose governed Slack capabilities instead of raw Slack operations

items.body: The product shall allow governed retrieval of recent Slack channel context and thread context using bounded results only. Read flows shall support channel-scoped context lookup and thread summarization without enabling raw workspace export or unrestricted message retrieval.
items.rationale: The evidence defines read capabilities for channel context and thread summarization and states that they produce summaries and data reads without raw data export or mutation.
items.title: Support bounded Slack context reads for channels and threads

items.body: The product shall support preparation of channel messages and structured incident updates as preview-only outputs. These flows shall stop before posting, return draft or preview content for review, and avoid any direct Slack mutation during preparation.
items.rationale: The capability evidence marks message preparation and incident update preparation as prepare-only with no approval execution or system mutation.
items.title: Prepare Slack messages and incident updates without sending them

items.body: The product shall enforce an approval boundary for announcement-style posting and any other governed posting flow. Before execution, the system shall return an approval-ready request or preview and shall not post to Slack until the required approval condition is satisfied.
items.rationale: The fronting intent explicitly includes approval-gated posting, and the evidence includes an announcement request capability that requires approval before posting, but final execution behavior is not yet evidenced.
items.title: Require approval before announcement or posting actions cross into execution

items.body: The product shall enforce channel allow-list rules on governed Slack reads, message preparation, incident update preparation, announcement requests, and any eventual posting action. Requests involving unauthorized channels shall be denied with a governed response rather than falling through to raw Slack access.
items.rationale: Channel allow-list enforcement is named directly in the fronting intent and project summary as a core governance requirement.
items.title: Enforce channel boundaries through allow-list policy

items.body: The product shall produce audit evidence for governed Slack reads, preview generation, approval requests, and denied actions. Audit records shall preserve enough traceability for reviewers to understand what capability was invoked, what boundary or policy applied, and why a request was allowed, stopped, or denied.
items.rationale: Audit evidence is called out in both the project summary and fronting intent, alongside explicit denial requirements.
items.title: Record audit evidence for governed actions and policy denials

items.body: The product shall explicitly refuse requests for raw Slack API access, raw MCP access, bulk export, and posting to channels outside authorized policy. Denial behavior shall be intentional and user-visible, not an accidental backend failure or silent omission.
items.rationale: The fronting intent specifically requires explicit denial of raw Slack API, MCP, bulk export, and unauthorized channel posting.
items.title: Explicitly deny raw export, raw tool access, and unauthorized posting

envelope.summary: Candidate PM-facing requirement blocks for a governed Slack fronting experience that exposes bounded read and prepare flows, enforces approval and channel boundaries, records audit evidence, and denies raw Slack access.

The brief explicitly requires denial of raw Slack tool access; requirements should avoid accidentally exposing raw API, MCP, or export-shaped outputs through convenience features.

Actor IDs exist in prior accepted context, but the source text does not fully specify which actor can approve versus only request.
Raw Slack API, MCP, and bulk export denial are explicit intent constraints and should appear as stop conditions, not hidden implementation details.

patches.rationale: Protects the governed-read posture and avoids accidental expansion into general Slack discovery or extraction.

value.rationale: The evidence emphasizes bounded channel and thread context, and the intent explicitly denies bulk export.

patches.rationale: Turns channel governance from an implied rule into a clear non-goal against broad posting capability.

value.rationale: Channel allow-list enforcement and unauthorized channel posting denial are named product constraints.

patches.rationale: Prevents PM confusion between draft generation and actual Slack mutation.

value.rationale: Current evidence supports prepare-only and request flows, with business effects that stop short of execution.
value.statement: Do not auto-send prepared messages, incident updates, or announcements without an explicit approval-gated execution step.

patches.rationale: Keeps the showcase focused on fronted communication workflows rather than broad operational control of Slack.

value.rationale: The documented scope is governed reads, message preparation, approval handling, channel boundaries, and audit evidence.
value.statement: Do not treat this showcase as a general Slack administration, moderation, or workspace management product.

patches.rationale: Captures a critical governance and security boundary in PM-readable form.

value.rationale: The integration evidence states that Studio stores secret references only and project exports must not contain tokens.

patches.rationale: Avoids overcommitting the product to enterprise-wide surveillance or universal Slack observability.

value.rationale: The brief calls for audit evidence in the context of governed channel reads, preparation, approvals, and boundary enforcement.

Channel boundaries and allow-list enforcement are core governance themes, so avoid wording that accidentally permits broad workspace-wide reads or posting.

patches.rationale: The brief centers the showcase on governed channel reads, thread inspection, and explicit denial of raw Slack access.

value.evidence: Scenario review shows representative channel-context and thread-summary requests return bounded results through the product, while raw Slack tool access is not exposed to agents.
value.statement: Users can retrieve bounded Slack channel context and thread summaries through governed product capabilities without needing raw Slack API or MCP access.

patches.rationale: Developer evidence explicitly supports prepare-only message and incident update capabilities with no system mutation.

value.evidence: Representative prepare flows produce message or incident-update previews, and review confirms the workflow does not execute a live Slack post at the preparation step.
value.statement: Users can prepare Slack channel messages and structured incident updates as previews that stop before sending.

patches.rationale: The source brief emphasizes approved message preparation and approval-gated posting, and the evidence supports an announcement request path.

value.statement: Channel announcements are handled through an approval-gated request flow rather than direct posting.

patches.rationale: Channel allow-list enforcement and unauthorized channel posting denial are explicit product goals in the fronting intent.

value.evidence: Verification review includes at least one disallowed-channel scenario showing the product blocks the action or stops the workflow according to channel governance rules.
value.statement: Channel boundary rules are enforced so users cannot prepare or request Slack actions for unauthorized channels.

patches.rationale: Audit evidence is called out directly in the project summary and fronting intent, so success should require observable review artifacts.

value.evidence: PM review can inspect audit records or equivalent trace evidence for representative read, prepare, request, and denial scenarios.
value.statement: Governed Slack interactions produce reviewable audit evidence for allowed actions and denied actions.

What specific audit evidence must PM be able to review for governed reads, message preparation, and denied actions?
Do you want separate success criteria for bounded channel reads versus thread summarization, or should they roll up into one governed read outcome?
What proof should count for channel allow-list enforcement: denied attempts, policy review, or both?
envelope.summary: PM-facing success criteria patch candidates derived from the Slack fronting brief and evidence. Focus is on governed Slack reads, prepare-only message workflows, approval-gated announcements, channel boundary enforcement, audit evidence, and denial of raw Slack access.
envelope.title: Success Criteria Proposal: Slack Fronting Showcase 0.2.0 RC4
watchouts: The fronting intent mentions approval-gated posting, but the supplied integration evidence only confirms prepare/request operations, not an execute/post capability.
The brief requires denial of raw Slack API and MCP access, but success criteria should be framed around observable product behavior and reviewable evidence, not internal architecture claims alone.
Channel allow-list enforcement is stated in intent but not detailed in the operation inputs, so evidence expectations may need clarification.

# Slack Integration Evidence Template

> Imported from starter template `slack-fronting-starter`. Verify every operation against your chosen native API/MCP source before locking.

Native API connection: SLACK_API_URL
MCP connection: SLACK_MCP_ENDPOINT

## Template-suggested backend operations
- slack.web.read_channel_context: GET /slack/channels/{channel_id}/context
- slack.web.summarize_thread: GET /slack/channels/{channel_id}/threads/{thread_ts}/summary
- slack.web.prepare_channel_message: POST /slack/channels/{channel_id}/messages/preview
- slack.web.prepare_incident_update: POST /slack/channels/{channel_id}/incident-updates/preview
- slack.web.request_channel_announcement: POST /slack/channels/{channel_id}/announcements/request

## Secret posture
- Studio stores secret references only; project exports must not contain tokens.
- Teams can implement either native API or MCP backend seams behind the same governed ANIP contract.

# Source 3: Slack Fronting Showcase 0.2.0 RC4 fronting intent
Kind: Fronting intent
Filename: slack-fronting-intent.md

# Slack Fronting Showcase 0.2.0 RC4 Fronting Intent

Create the canonical Slack governed fronting showcase. The ANIP service must expose business capabilities for bounded Slack channel context, message and thread inspection, approved message preparation, approval-gated posting, channel allow-list enforcement, audit evidence, and explicit denial of raw Slack API, MCP, bulk export, and unauthorized channel posting. Agents should see governed Slack capabilities, not raw Slack Web API or MCP operations.

## Next review step
Add integration evidence from a real source before contract generation. Use uploaded OpenAPI, GraphQL, MCP schema, API docs, permission matrices, live discovery, or manual operation entry. Studio must not invent backend operations from the project name or domain.

source_documents.title: Slack Fronting Showcase 0.2.0 RC4 fronting intent

data.approval_posture_summary: Read capabilities operate in a read-only posture. Communication-related capabilities operate in a prepare-only, approval-oriented posture that stops before direct mutation, with one-time or session-bound grants used for preview/request flows where supported.
business_goals: Enable bounded Slack channel and thread reads for declared context without exposing raw workspace tools.
Support preparation of channel messages, incident updates, and announcement requests without sending them directly.
Enforce channel allow-list and unauthorized posting boundaries through the product experience.
Preserve approval gates and audit evidence for communication-related actions.
Present business-facing governed Slack capabilities rather than raw Slack Web API or MCP operations.
data.business_problem: Teams need Slack access for context gathering and communication drafting, but raw Slack API or MCP access can bypass channel restrictions, approval controls, and auditability. The product addresses this by exposing governed business capabilities instead of unrestricted Slack operations.
data.governed_behavior_summary: The product should return bounded Slack context for approved read scenarios, prepare communication previews or requests for write-adjacent scenarios, enforce channel and posting boundaries, produce audit evidence, and explicitly deny raw Slack API, MCP, bulk export, or unauthorized posting paths.
data.lock_rationale: Lockable for PM summary framing because the brief consistently defines the governed Slack use cases, boundaries, and approval posture. One open scope question remains on whether actual posting execution should be represented now or deferred until explicit execution evidence exists.
data.product_purpose: Provide a governed Slack fronting experience that lets users read bounded channel and thread context, prepare channel communications for review, and work within explicit approval, channel, and audit boundaries instead of using raw Slack tools directly.

actors.action_expectations: Can request channel context, summarize threads, prepare channel messages, prepare incident updates, and request channel announcements through governed Slack capabilities.
actors.approval_expectations: Can complete read actions directly. Can prepare drafts and announcement requests, but should stop at the approval boundary before any posting or execution step.
actors.notes: Derived from the brief's governed reads, approved message preparation, and explicit denial of raw Slack tool access. Title should be replaced if the business uses a more specific role name.
actors.summary: Business user who needs governed Slack channel and thread context, and who prepares messages or incident updates without directly posting to Slack.
actors.title: Slack Requester
actors.visibility_expectations: Can see bounded Slack channel context, thread summaries, message previews, incident update previews, and approval-request previews within allowed channels. Should not see raw Slack API or MCP operations, bulk export surfaces, or unrestricted workspace data.

actors.action_expectations: Can review approval-gated Slack posting requests and decide whether they may proceed, be revised, or be denied.
actors.approval_expectations: Owns the explicit approval boundary for posting-related actions described as approval-gated in the brief, but the exact scope by action type is still unclear.
actors.notes: The source names approval-gated posting and announcement requests that require approval before posting, but does not specify the exact business role or whether approval differs across announcements, incident updates, and other messages.
actors.summary: Business reviewer who evaluates approval-gated Slack posting requests before any governed message is allowed to proceed toward posting.
actors.title: Slack Approver
actors.visibility_expectations: Can see the prepared message, announcement, or incident update preview plus enough channel context and audit evidence to make an approval decision within governed boundaries.

actors.action_expectations: Can request channel context, summarize threads, prepare channel messages, prepare incident updates, and request channel announcements through governed Slack capabilities.

actors.visibility_expectations: Can see bounded Slack channel context, thread summaries, message previews, incident update previews, and approval-request previews within allowed channels. Should not see raw Slack API or MCP operations, bulk export surfaces, or unrestricted workspace data.

actors.action_expectations: Can review approval-gated Slack posting requests and decide whether they may proceed, be revised, or be denied.
actors.approval_expectations: Owns the explicit approval boundary for posting-related actions described as approval-gated in the brief, but the exact scope by action type is still unclear.
actors.notes: The source names approval-gated posting and announcement requests that require approval before posting, but does not specify the exact business role or whether approval differs across announcements, incident updates, and other messages.
actors.summary: Business reviewer who evaluates approval-gated Slack posting requests before any governed message is allowed to proceed toward posting.

actors.visibility_expectations: Can see the prepared message, announcement, or incident update preview plus enough channel context and audit evidence to make an approval decision within governed boundaries.

data.policy_summary: Slack access should be mediated through governed fronting capabilities: bounded channel and thread context may be returned directly, message and incident workflows stop at preparation, announcement flows require approval, and raw Slack access patterns remain outside allowed business use.

rules.governed_outcome: Return bounded recent channel context or thread summary for the declared Slack context without exposing raw workspace tooling.
rules.notes: Aligned to bounded channel reads and thread inspection in the source brief.

rules.governed_outcome: Prepare a Slack message preview or draft for review without sending it.
Prepare a structured incident update preview for a Slack channel and stop before sending.

rules.governed_outcome: Prepare the channel announcement request and stop for explicit approval before any posting can occur.
rules.notes: Represents approval-gated posting through the announcement request flow.

data.approval_posture_summary: Read capabilities operate in a read-only posture. Communication-related capabilities operate in a prepare-only, approval-oriented posture that stops before direct mutation, with one-time or session-bound grants used for preview/request flows where supported.
business_goals: Enable bounded Slack channel and thread reads for declared context without exposing raw workspace tools.
Support preparation of channel messages, incident updates, and announcement requests without sending them directly.
Enforce channel allow-list and unauthorized posting boundaries through the product experience.
Preserve approval gates and audit evidence for communication-related actions.
Present business-facing governed Slack capabilities rather than raw Slack Web API or MCP operations.
data.business_problem: Teams need Slack access for context gathering and communication drafting, but raw Slack API or MCP access can bypass channel restrictions, approval controls, and auditability. The product addresses this by exposing governed business capabilities instead of unrestricted Slack operations.
data.governed_behavior_summary: The product should return bounded Slack context for approved read scenarios, prepare communication previews or requests for write-adjacent scenarios, enforce channel and posting boundaries, produce audit evidence, and explicitly deny raw Slack API, MCP, bulk export, or unauthorized posting paths.
data.lock_rationale: Lockable for PM summary framing because the brief consistently defines the governed Slack use cases, boundaries, and approval posture. One open scope question remains on whether actual posting execution should be represented now or deferred until explicit execution evidence exists.
data.product_purpose: Provide a governed Slack fronting experience that lets users read bounded channel and thread context, prepare channel communications for review, and work within explicit approval, channel, and audit boundaries instead of using raw Slack tools directly.

entries.evidence: Scenario review shows representative channel-context and thread-summary requests return bounded results through the product, while raw Slack tool access is not exposed to agents.
entries.statement: Users can retrieve bounded Slack channel context and thread summaries through governed product capabilities without needing raw Slack API or MCP access.

items.body: Bind runtime access so callers may invoke only the governed capability set: slack.channel.read_context, slack.thread.summarize, slack.message.prepare, slack.incident_update.prepare, and slack.announcement.request. Any request for raw Slack API operations, raw MCP operations, bulk export, direct workspace tool access, or generic passthrough execution must return an explicit denied policy outcome with guidance to use the governed capability that matches the user intent. Do not silently fall through to native Slack tooling.
items.rationale: The locked baseline and fronting intent explicitly require denial of raw Slack API, MCP, bulk export, and direct tool access, while exposing only business-facing governed capabilities.
items.title: Expose governed Slack capabilities only and deny raw integration surfaces

items.body: Bind every capability that accepts channel_id to a channel authorization check before execution. If the requested channel is outside the approved boundary, stop the flow with an explicit denied outcome that states channel governance as the reason. Apply the same boundary consistently to bounded channel reads, thread summarization, message preview preparation, incident update preparation, announcement requests, and any later posting path derived from them.
items.rationale: Channel allow-list enforcement is a core requirement and is called out across scenarios for reads, preparation flows, announcement handling, and unauthorized posting denial.
items.title: Enforce channel allow-list boundaries across reads and write-adjacent flows

items.body: Bind slack.channel.read_context and slack.thread.summarize to read_only runtime posture. Responses must stay bounded to recent channel context or the requested thread context and must not expose raw workspace export behavior. Require concrete channel reference for channel reads and both channel and thread references for thread summarization. Requests attempting to broaden scope into unrestricted retrieval or export should be denied as out of governed scope.
items.rationale: The developer evidence formalizes these capabilities as read_only with no raw_data_export or system.mutation, and the scenarios require bounded context retrieval without raw workspace access.
items.title: Keep read capabilities bounded, read-only, and non-exporting

items.body: Bind slack.message.prepare and slack.incident_update.prepare to prepare_only runtime behavior. Successful execution may produce draft or preview artifacts only and must stop before any Slack mutation. The runtime outcome should make the stop-before-send boundary explicit. For incident updates, require structured inputs including channel_id, incident_id, status, and summary, and restrict status to investigating, identified, monitoring, or resolved.
items.rationale: The baseline requires preparation without sending, and the developer evidence marks both capabilities as prepare_only with no approval.execute or system.mutation. Incident update input constraints are explicitly defined.
items.title: Bind message and incident update preparation to preview-only outcomes

items.body: Bind slack.announcement.request to prepare_only behavior that creates an approval-ready request or preview artifact and stops pending approval. The runtime outcome should be require_approval or equivalent approval-stopped status rather than success-as-posted. No Slack post should occur from this capability without a separate approved execution path. Preserve enough request context for later reviewer and approver inspection.
items.rationale: The baseline and scenario explicitly require approval before announcement posting and describe announcement handling as a governed request that stops until approval is granted.
items.title: Treat announcement handling as approval-gated request creation, not posting

items.body: Bind missing required references to clarification_required rather than denied when the user intent is otherwise within governed scope. At minimum, missing channel_id for channel-scoped capabilities and missing thread_ts for thread summarization should trigger clarification. Use denied only when the request asks for disallowed raw access, targets an unauthorized channel, or attempts posting outside approved policy. Keep these outcomes distinct in runtime responses and audit evidence.
items.rationale: The reviewed input contracts indicate clarify behavior for required references, while the product baseline separately emphasizes intentional denials for raw access and unauthorized channels.
items.title: Separate missing-input clarification from policy denial

items.body: Bind runtime execution to signed trust mode, scoped authority, purpose binding, and delegation tokens. Each governed outcome—allowed read, preview generated, approval stop, or denial—should emit durable searchable audit evidence including capability invoked, high-level policy outcome, applicable boundary or approval rule, and lineage identifiers: task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id when present. Audit records must support later review without exposing raw Slack credentials or unrestricted backend details.
items.rationale: The locked requirements explicitly require signed trust mode, scoped authority, delegation tokens, durable searchable audit, and lineage fields, while scenarios require reviewable evidence for governed outcomes.
items.title: Require signed delegated invocation context and durable audit evidence

items.body: Treat backend_options as internal or tightly restricted runtime input. If exposed at all, only allow a reviewed safe subset that cannot expand scope, invoke raw Slack operations, disable channel checks, or alter prepare_only and approval boundaries. Unrecognized or privileged backend_options should be rejected with a governed denial rather than passed through.
items.rationale: backend_options appears in developer evidence for all capabilities, but the product intent strongly prohibits raw tool exposure and governance bypass through generic backend controls.
items.title: Constrain backend_options so they cannot bypass governance

questions_for_user: What is the source of truth for the Slack channel allow-list at runtime, and is it shared across all five governed capabilities?
Should approval be required only for announcement requests, or also for any later execution step derived from message or incident-update previews?
Do you want explicit clarification_required behavior when channel_id or thread_ts is missing or ambiguous, or should those cases be treated as immediate denial?
Should backend_options be fully denied to external callers unless explicitly whitelisted, given the product intent to avoid raw tool exposure?
What minimum audit fields must be emitted for allowed, denied, preview-only, and approval-stopped outcomes beyond task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id?
envelope.summary: Candidate runtime policy binding blocks for governed Slack reads, preview-only preparation, approval-gated announcement requests, channel boundary enforcement, raw-access denial, and audit evidence. Derived from the locked baseline and current capability inventory; intended for explicit review before acceptance.
envelope.title: Runtime Policy Binding Proposal: Slack Fronting Showcase 0.2.0 RC4
watchouts: The baseline says production_single_service, but the current service shape is multi_service; runtime bindings should avoid assuming final deployment topology.
The baseline requires signed trust mode, but no concrete signature verification or token-binding mechanism is defined here.
Channel allow-list enforcement is required, but the allow-list mechanism and ownership are not defined in source evidence.
The product explicitly denies raw Slack API, MCP, and bulk export access; runtime bindings should not leave escape hatches through backend_options or generic passthrough fields.
Preview-only capabilities must not mutate Slack; bindings should distinguish prepare_only from any future execution path.
Approval behavior is explicit for announcement requests, but the approver identity and approval system are not defined in the baseline.

next_steps: Confirm whether the template Slack HTTP operations are acceptable as provisional backend bindings for review.
Choose the enforcement location for channel allow-list and raw-access denial outcomes.
Define the audit evidence sink and minimum stored fields for lineage, policy outcome, and capability invocation.
Decide whether backend_options is supported externally, narrowed, or removed from generated bindings.
Review and accept, edit, or reject the candidate blocks below before persistence.

items.body: Bind the Slack Fronting service to the reviewed Slack operations for bounded reads and preview generation only: GET /slack/channels/{channel_id}/context for channel context, GET /slack/channels/{channel_id}/threads/{thread_ts}/summary for thread summarization, POST /slack/channels/{channel_id}/messages/preview for message preparation, and POST /slack/channels/{channel_id}/incident-updates/preview for incident update preparation. Preserve the capability-to-operation mapping exactly and do not add generic Slack passthrough methods. Treat read_context and summarize as read-only bindings, and treat both prepare capabilities as preview-only bindings with no send side effect.
items.rationale: The locked capability inventory and developer evidence provide direct backend_operation mappings and execution posture for these four capabilities.
items.title: Bind Slack Fronting capabilities to explicit bounded Slack operations

items.body: Bind the announcement request capability to POST /slack/channels/{channel_id}/announcements/request as a distinct governance path. The binding should create an approval-ready request artifact and stop before any Slack post occurs. Keep this operation separate from message preview and any future posting path so approval-required behavior remains explicit in runtime handling and audit evidence.
items.rationale: The baseline and scenario evidence make announcement handling a separate governed capability with an explicit approval boundary and a dedicated backend operation.
items.title: Bind announcement requests to a separate approval-gated backend operation

items.body: Insert a mandatory policy enforcement seam ahead of every Slack-bound backend call. This seam should validate channel allow-list eligibility, reject unauthorized channels, and explicitly deny requests that attempt raw Slack API access, MCP access, bulk export, or other bypass behavior. Denials should return governed outcomes rather than adapter errors, and no downstream Slack operation should be invoked after a denial decision.
items.rationale: Channel allow-list enforcement and explicit denial of raw access are core locked requirements and appear across multiple scenarios as first-class runtime outcomes.
items.title: Enforce channel boundary and raw-access denial before downstream Slack calls

items.body: For every allowed, denied, or approval-stopped request, emit durable searchable audit evidence at the backend binding layer or immediately around it. Record at minimum the invoked capability, backend operation name, service handling the request, policy outcome, high-level reason, channel reference, and required lineage fields: task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id when present. Ensure audit review stays capability-oriented and does not expose raw Slack credentials or unrestricted tool details.
items.rationale: The locked baseline requires durable searchable audit plus lineage fields, and the scenarios require reviewable evidence for reads, previews, denials, and approval stops.
items.title: Bind all governed operations to durable searchable audit recording

items.body: Configure backend bindings so downstream Slack access is performed under scoped delegated authority rather than broad workspace credentials exposed to callers. Preserve purpose binding, scoped authority, and delegation token expectations in the adapter contract, and ensure generated integrations never surface raw Slack tokens, raw tool handles, or unrestricted workspace access to agents or end users. Authentication details should be explicit in deployment configuration, but secret material must remain reference-only.
items.rationale: The requirements specify purpose binding, scoped authority, delegation tokens, and signed trust mode, but the exact token format and adapter auth mechanism are not yet defined.
items.title: Make downstream Slack binding use signed delegated authority with scoped purpose

items.body: Use the reviewed capability input contracts as the only externally supported request shape for backend invocation. Required identifiers such as channel_id, thread_ts, incident_id, status, text, summary, and announcement should be validated before adapter calls. For incident updates, enforce the supported status values exactly: investigating, identified, monitoring, resolved. Treat backend_options as a guarded implementation field rather than an open passthrough unless a reviewed allow-list is defined, to avoid accidental exposure of raw Slack or MCP controls.
items.rationale: The source includes reviewed input contracts and explicit allowed values, while backend_options is present but otherwise unspecified and therefore risky as an unrestricted passthrough.
items.title: Bind only the reviewed input contracts and constrain backend_options usage

questions_for_user: Should Slack Fronting and Slack Governance call the same downstream Slack adapter, or should announcement requests use a separate governed adapter path?
Do you want channel allow-list enforcement implemented inside each service, through a shared policy component, or both?
Should backend_options remain pass-through to the adapter, or be restricted to an internal-only field with an allow-list?
What concrete audit sink or storage target should durable searchable evidence bind to?
Should approval-required announcement requests stop after creating a review artifact only, or also bind to an approval system record at request time?
Are the template Slack endpoints intended as the real backend contract for this release, or only placeholders pending real integration evidence?
envelope.summary: Candidate backend-binding blocks for the governed Slack fronting services, grounded in the locked baseline and current Slack evidence. These blocks focus on explicit downstream operation bindings, policy enforcement seams, and audit wiring without expanding scope beyond the approved capability inventory.
envelope.title: Backend Binding Proposal: Slack Fronting Showcase 0.2.0 RC4
watchouts: Current backend operations come from template evidence and should be verified against a real Slack integration source before locking.
The locked baseline requires explicit denial of raw Slack API, MCP, and bulk export access; backend bindings must not expose generic proxy or passthrough operations.
Service shape notes say the topology is still reviewable; binding decisions should not hard-code cross-service handoffs that may change.
Prepare flows are preview-only and must not bind to posting or mutation endpoints by accident.
Channel allow-list enforcement is required across reads, preparation, announcement requests, and any later posting path; inconsistent enforcement points will create policy gaps.
Durable searchable audit is required, but the evidence schema and storage system are not yet defined.

next_steps: Review and accept, edit, or reject the candidate verification blocks individually.
Decide whether verification must cover both capability contracts and cross-service approval/audit flows.
Add real integration evidence and any minimum audit record fields before finalizing verification expectations.
Resolve whether service topology remains multi-service or is collapsed to match the stated scale preference.

items.body: For each locked capability, verify required inputs, declared execution posture, and bounded outcome behavior. slack.channel.read_context must require a concrete channel reference and return bounded recent context only. slack.thread.summarize must require both channel_id and thread_ts and remain inspection-only. slack.message.prepare, slack.incident_update.prepare, and slack.announcement.request must return preview or request artifacts and stop before any Slack mutation. Verification should confirm that outputs align to governed business capabilities rather than raw backend operations.
items.rationale: The baseline and developer evidence are explicit about the five canonical capabilities, their inputs, and their read-only or prepare-only posture.
items.title: Verify each governed Slack capability against its bounded contract

items.body: Verification should prove that preparation and announcement flows do not post to Slack as part of the initial request. slack.message.prepare and slack.incident_update.prepare must stop at preview generation. slack.announcement.request must stop at an approval boundary and preserve a reviewable request artifact. Tests should treat any implicit posting, hidden side effect, or automatic execution as a failure.
items.rationale: The locked requirements and scenarios repeatedly require preview-only behavior and approval-gated posting, with no direct mutation during preparation.
items.title: Verify explicit stop-before-send and approval-required boundaries

items.body: Verification should cover allowed and denied channel cases across reads, message preparation, incident update preparation, announcement requests, and any posting-adjacent path. Requests for channels outside the governed boundary must be denied before preview or execution continues. Denials should be explicit, policy-based, and user-visible, not generic backend failures. Verification should also confirm there is no fallback to unrestricted Slack access when a channel is unauthorized.
items.rationale: Channel allow-list enforcement and unauthorized channel denial are core baseline requirements and have dedicated scenarios.
items.title: Verify channel boundary enforcement and governed denial behavior

items.body: Verification should include negative tests showing that requests for raw Slack API access, raw MCP access, bulk export, or equivalent unrestricted workspace operations are intentionally refused. The refusal should direct actors toward governed capabilities such as bounded channel context, thread summarization, or preparation flows. Verification should fail if the system silently ignores the request, proxies raw operations, or exposes native integration surfaces to agents or end users.
items.rationale: The fronting intent and requirements explicitly call for denial of raw Slack API, MCP, bulk export, and direct workspace tool access.
items.title: Verify explicit refusal of raw Slack API, MCP, and bulk export paths

items.body: Verification should prove that governed reads, thread inspection, preview generation, approval stops, and denials all produce durable and searchable audit evidence. At minimum, evidence should let a reviewer determine which governed capability ran, the request outcome, what boundary or approval rule applied, and why the flow was allowed, stopped, or denied. Verification should also confirm lineage fields are preserved for task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id where applicable, without exposing raw Slack credentials or unrestricted tooling details.
items.rationale: Audit durability, searchability, and lineage are explicit requirements, and audit review is a named scenario in the baseline.
items.title: Verify durable, searchable audit evidence for governed outcomes

items.body: Verification should confirm that required inputs trigger clarification or rejection rather than best-effort guessing. channel_id is required for all channel-scoped capabilities; thread_ts is required for thread summarization; incident updates must require incident_id, status, and summary; status must be constrained to investigating, identified, monitoring, or resolved. Optional fields should remain optional and not change the governed posture. Verification should also confirm that policy checks happen before backend execution for unauthorized channels and disallowed raw-access requests.
items.rationale: Developer evidence and drafted input contracts define required fields and allowed status values, while the baseline requires governed preflight and restricted-vs-denied behavior.
items.title: Verify required input handling and constrained policy checks

questions_for_user: Should verification require capability-level tests only, or also end-to-end checks across Slack Fronting and Slack Governance?
Do you want audit verification to assert a minimum evidence schema, or only presence of reviewable trace records?
Should channel allow-list verification assume a static policy fixture for now, since the exact allow-list mechanism is not yet defined?
Do you want explicit negative tests for every capability against raw Slack API, MCP, and bulk export requests?
envelope.summary: Candidate developer-facing verification expectation blocks derived from the locked Slack fronting baseline, scenario set, and reviewed capability evidence. Focus is on proving governed bounded reads, preview-only preparation, approval stops, channel boundary enforcement, raw-access denial, and audit traceability.
envelope.title: Verification Expectations Proposal: Slack Fronting Showcase 0.2.0 RC4
watchouts: The baseline requires a production single-service preference, but the current reviewed shape is multi-service; verification should avoid hard-coding topology assumptions until that is resolved.
Audit evidence is required and reviewable, but the exact evidence schema is not defined in the source.
Channel allow-list enforcement is required, but the concrete allow-list mechanism is not yet specified.
Integration evidence still references template backend operations and explicitly says real-source evidence should be added before contract generation.

accepted_payload.body: Bind runtime access so callers may invoke only the governed capability set: slack.channel.read_context, slack.thread.summarize, slack.message.prepare, slack.incident_update.prepare, and slack.announcement.request. Any request for raw Slack API operations, raw MCP operations, bulk export, direct workspace tool access, or generic passthrough execution must return an explicit denied policy outcome with guidance to use the governed capability that matches the user intent. Do not silently fall through to native Slack tooling.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked baseline and fronting intent explicitly require denial of raw Slack API, MCP, bulk export, and direct tool access, while exposing only business-facing governed capabilities.
accepted_payload.title: Expose governed Slack capabilities only and deny raw integration surfaces

accepted_payload.body: Bind every capability that accepts channel_id to a channel authorization check before execution. If the requested channel is outside the approved boundary, stop the flow with an explicit denied outcome that states channel governance as the reason. Apply the same boundary consistently to bounded channel reads, thread summarization, message preview preparation, incident update preparation, announcement requests, and any later posting path derived from them.
accepted_payload.rationale: Channel allow-list enforcement is a core requirement and is called out across scenarios for reads, preparation flows, announcement handling, and unauthorized posting denial.
accepted_payload.title: Enforce channel allow-list boundaries across reads and write-adjacent flows

accepted_payload.body: Bind slack.channel.read_context and slack.thread.summarize to read_only runtime posture. Responses must stay bounded to recent channel context or the requested thread context and must not expose raw workspace export behavior. Require concrete channel reference for channel reads and both channel and thread references for thread summarization. Requests attempting to broaden scope into unrestricted retrieval or export should be denied as out of governed scope.
accepted_payload.rationale: The developer evidence formalizes these capabilities as read_only with no raw_data_export or system.mutation, and the scenarios require bounded context retrieval without raw workspace access.
accepted_payload.title: Keep read capabilities bounded, read-only, and non-exporting

accepted_payload.body: Bind slack.message.prepare and slack.incident_update.prepare to prepare_only runtime behavior. Successful execution may produce draft or preview artifacts only and must stop before any Slack mutation. The runtime outcome should make the stop-before-send boundary explicit. For incident updates, require structured inputs including channel_id, incident_id, status, and summary, and restrict status to investigating, identified, monitoring, or resolved.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline requires preparation without sending, and the developer evidence marks both capabilities as prepare_only with no approval.execute or system.mutation. Incident update input constraints are explicitly defined.
accepted_payload.title: Bind message and incident update preparation to preview-only outcomes

accepted_payload.body: Bind slack.announcement.request to prepare_only behavior that creates an approval-ready request or preview artifact and stops pending approval. The runtime outcome should be require_approval or equivalent approval-stopped status rather than success-as-posted. No Slack post should occur from this capability without a separate approved execution path. Preserve enough request context for later reviewer and approver inspection.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline and scenario explicitly require approval before announcement posting and describe announcement handling as a governed request that stops until approval is granted.
accepted_payload.title: Treat announcement handling as approval-gated request creation, not posting

accepted_payload.body: Bind missing required references to clarification_required rather than denied when the user intent is otherwise within governed scope. At minimum, missing channel_id for channel-scoped capabilities and missing thread_ts for thread summarization should trigger clarification. Use denied only when the request asks for disallowed raw access, targets an unauthorized channel, or attempts posting outside approved policy. Keep these outcomes distinct in runtime responses and audit evidence.
accepted_payload.rationale: The reviewed input contracts indicate clarify behavior for required references, while the product baseline separately emphasizes intentional denials for raw access and unauthorized channels.
accepted_payload.title: Separate missing-input clarification from policy denial

accepted_payload.body: Bind runtime execution to signed trust mode, scoped authority, purpose binding, and delegation tokens. Each governed outcome—allowed read, preview generated, approval stop, or denial—should emit durable searchable audit evidence including capability invoked, high-level policy outcome, applicable boundary or approval rule, and lineage identifiers: task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id when present. Audit records must support later review without exposing raw Slack credentials or unrestricted backend details.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked requirements explicitly require signed trust mode, scoped authority, delegation tokens, durable searchable audit, and lineage fields, while scenarios require reviewable evidence for governed outcomes.
accepted_payload.title: Require signed delegated invocation context and durable audit evidence

accepted_payload.body: Treat backend_options as internal or tightly restricted runtime input. If exposed at all, only allow a reviewed safe subset that cannot expand scope, invoke raw Slack operations, disable channel checks, or alter prepare_only and approval boundaries. Unrecognized or privileged backend_options should be rejected with a governed denial rather than passed through.
accepted_payload.rationale: backend_options appears in developer evidence for all capabilities, but the product intent strongly prohibits raw tool exposure and governance bypass through generic backend controls.
accepted_payload.title: Constrain backend_options so they cannot bypass governance

accepted_payload.body: Bind the Slack Fronting service to the reviewed Slack operations for bounded reads and preview generation only: GET /slack/channels/{channel_id}/context for channel context, GET /slack/channels/{channel_id}/threads/{thread_ts}/summary for thread summarization, POST /slack/channels/{channel_id}/messages/preview for message preparation, and POST /slack/channels/{channel_id}/incident-updates/preview for incident update preparation. Preserve the capability-to-operation mapping exactly and do not add generic Slack passthrough methods. Treat read_context and summarize as read-only bindings, and treat both prepare capabilities as preview-only bindings with no send side effect.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked capability inventory and developer evidence provide direct backend_operation mappings and execution posture for these four capabilities.
accepted_payload.title: Bind Slack Fronting capabilities to explicit bounded Slack operations

accepted_payload.body: Bind the announcement request capability to POST /slack/channels/{channel_id}/announcements/request as a distinct governance path. The binding should create an approval-ready request artifact and stop before any Slack post occurs. Keep this operation separate from message preview and any future posting path so approval-required behavior remains explicit in runtime handling and audit evidence.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline and scenario evidence make announcement handling a separate governed capability with an explicit approval boundary and a dedicated backend operation.
accepted_payload.title: Bind announcement requests to a separate approval-gated backend operation

accepted_payload.body: Insert a mandatory policy enforcement seam ahead of every Slack-bound backend call. This seam should validate channel allow-list eligibility, reject unauthorized channels, and explicitly deny requests that attempt raw Slack API access, MCP access, bulk export, or other bypass behavior. Denials should return governed outcomes rather than adapter errors, and no downstream Slack operation should be invoked after a denial decision.
accepted_payload.rationale: Channel allow-list enforcement and explicit denial of raw access are core locked requirements and appear across multiple scenarios as first-class runtime outcomes.
accepted_payload.title: Enforce channel boundary and raw-access denial before downstream Slack calls

accepted_payload.body: For every allowed, denied, or approval-stopped request, emit durable searchable audit evidence at the backend binding layer or immediately around it. Record at minimum the invoked capability, backend operation name, service handling the request, policy outcome, high-level reason, channel reference, and required lineage fields: task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id when present. Ensure audit review stays capability-oriented and does not expose raw Slack credentials or unrestricted tool details.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked baseline requires durable searchable audit plus lineage fields, and the scenarios require reviewable evidence for reads, previews, denials, and approval stops.
accepted_payload.title: Bind all governed operations to durable searchable audit recording

accepted_payload.body: Configure backend bindings so downstream Slack access is performed under scoped delegated authority rather than broad workspace credentials exposed to callers. Preserve purpose binding, scoped authority, and delegation token expectations in the adapter contract, and ensure generated integrations never surface raw Slack tokens, raw tool handles, or unrestricted workspace access to agents or end users. Authentication details should be explicit in deployment configuration, but secret material must remain reference-only.
accepted_payload.rationale: The requirements specify purpose binding, scoped authority, delegation tokens, and signed trust mode, but the exact token format and adapter auth mechanism are not yet defined.
accepted_payload.title: Make downstream Slack binding use signed delegated authority with scoped purpose

accepted_payload.body: Use the reviewed capability input contracts as the only externally supported request shape for backend invocation. Required identifiers such as channel_id, thread_ts, incident_id, status, text, summary, and announcement should be validated before adapter calls. For incident updates, enforce the supported status values exactly: investigating, identified, monitoring, resolved. Treat backend_options as a guarded implementation field rather than an open passthrough unless a reviewed allow-list is defined, to avoid accidental exposure of raw Slack or MCP controls.
accepted_payload.rationale: The source includes reviewed input contracts and explicit allowed values, while backend_options is present but otherwise unspecified and therefore risky as an unrestricted passthrough.
accepted_payload.title: Bind only the reviewed input contracts and constrain backend_options usage

accepted_payload.body: For each locked capability, verify required inputs, declared execution posture, and bounded outcome behavior. slack.channel.read_context must require a concrete channel reference and return bounded recent context only. slack.thread.summarize must require both channel_id and thread_ts and remain inspection-only. slack.message.prepare, slack.incident_update.prepare, and slack.announcement.request must return preview or request artifacts and stop before any Slack mutation. Verification should confirm that outputs align to governed business capabilities rather than raw backend operations.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline and developer evidence are explicit about the five canonical capabilities, their inputs, and their read-only or prepare-only posture.
accepted_payload.title: Verify each governed Slack capability against its bounded contract

accepted_payload.body: Verification should prove that preparation and announcement flows do not post to Slack as part of the initial request. slack.message.prepare and slack.incident_update.prepare must stop at preview generation. slack.announcement.request must stop at an approval boundary and preserve a reviewable request artifact. Tests should treat any implicit posting, hidden side effect, or automatic execution as a failure.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked requirements and scenarios repeatedly require preview-only behavior and approval-gated posting, with no direct mutation during preparation.
accepted_payload.title: Verify explicit stop-before-send and approval-required boundaries

accepted_payload.body: Verification should cover allowed and denied channel cases across reads, message preparation, incident update preparation, announcement requests, and any posting-adjacent path. Requests for channels outside the governed boundary must be denied before preview or execution continues. Denials should be explicit, policy-based, and user-visible, not generic backend failures. Verification should also confirm there is no fallback to unrestricted Slack access when a channel is unauthorized.
accepted_payload.rationale: Channel allow-list enforcement and unauthorized channel denial are core baseline requirements and have dedicated scenarios.
accepted_payload.title: Verify channel boundary enforcement and governed denial behavior

accepted_payload.body: Verification should include negative tests showing that requests for raw Slack API access, raw MCP access, bulk export, or equivalent unrestricted workspace operations are intentionally refused. The refusal should direct actors toward governed capabilities such as bounded channel context, thread summarization, or preparation flows. Verification should fail if the system silently ignores the request, proxies raw operations, or exposes native integration surfaces to agents or end users.
accepted_payload.rationale: The fronting intent and requirements explicitly call for denial of raw Slack API, MCP, bulk export, and direct workspace tool access.
accepted_payload.title: Verify explicit refusal of raw Slack API, MCP, and bulk export paths

accepted_payload.body: Verification should prove that governed reads, thread inspection, preview generation, approval stops, and denials all produce durable and searchable audit evidence. At minimum, evidence should let a reviewer determine which governed capability ran, the request outcome, what boundary or approval rule applied, and why the flow was allowed, stopped, or denied. Verification should also confirm lineage fields are preserved for task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id where applicable, without exposing raw Slack credentials or unrestricted tooling details.
accepted_payload.rationale: Audit durability, searchability, and lineage are explicit requirements, and audit review is a named scenario in the baseline.
accepted_payload.title: Verify durable, searchable audit evidence for governed outcomes

accepted_payload.body: Verification should confirm that required inputs trigger clarification or rejection rather than best-effort guessing. channel_id is required for all channel-scoped capabilities; thread_ts is required for thread summarization; incident updates must require incident_id, status, and summary; status must be constrained to investigating, identified, monitoring, or resolved. Optional fields should remain optional and not change the governed posture. Verification should also confirm that policy checks happen before backend execution for unauthorized channels and disallowed raw-access requests.
accepted_payload.rationale: Developer evidence and drafted input contracts define required fields and allowed status values, while the baseline requires governed preflight and restricted-vs-denied behavior.
accepted_payload.title: Verify required input handling and constrained policy checks

items.body: For each locked capability, verify required inputs, declared execution posture, and bounded outcome behavior. slack.channel.read_context must require a concrete channel reference and return bounded recent context only. slack.thread.summarize must require both channel_id and thread_ts and remain inspection-only. slack.message.prepare, slack.incident_update.prepare, and slack.announcement.request must return preview or request artifacts and stop before any Slack mutation. Verification should confirm that outputs align to governed business capabilities rather than raw backend operations.
items.rationale: The baseline and developer evidence are explicit about the five canonical capabilities, their inputs, and their read-only or prepare-only posture.
items.title: Verify each governed Slack capability against its bounded contract

items.body: Verification should prove that preparation and announcement flows do not post to Slack as part of the initial request. slack.message.prepare and slack.incident_update.prepare must stop at preview generation. slack.announcement.request must stop at an approval boundary and preserve a reviewable request artifact. Tests should treat any implicit posting, hidden side effect, or automatic execution as a failure.
items.rationale: The locked requirements and scenarios repeatedly require preview-only behavior and approval-gated posting, with no direct mutation during preparation.
items.title: Verify explicit stop-before-send and approval-required boundaries

items.body: Verification should cover allowed and denied channel cases across reads, message preparation, incident update preparation, announcement requests, and any posting-adjacent path. Requests for channels outside the governed boundary must be denied before preview or execution continues. Denials should be explicit, policy-based, and user-visible, not generic backend failures. Verification should also confirm there is no fallback to unrestricted Slack access when a channel is unauthorized.
items.rationale: Channel allow-list enforcement and unauthorized channel denial are core baseline requirements and have dedicated scenarios.
items.title: Verify channel boundary enforcement and governed denial behavior

items.body: Verification should include negative tests showing that requests for raw Slack API access, raw MCP access, bulk export, or equivalent unrestricted workspace operations are intentionally refused. The refusal should direct actors toward governed capabilities such as bounded channel context, thread summarization, or preparation flows. Verification should fail if the system silently ignores the request, proxies raw operations, or exposes native integration surfaces to agents or end users.
items.rationale: The fronting intent and requirements explicitly call for denial of raw Slack API, MCP, bulk export, and direct workspace tool access.
items.title: Verify explicit refusal of raw Slack API, MCP, and bulk export paths

items.body: Verification should prove that governed reads, thread inspection, preview generation, approval stops, and denials all produce durable and searchable audit evidence. At minimum, evidence should let a reviewer determine which governed capability ran, the request outcome, what boundary or approval rule applied, and why the flow was allowed, stopped, or denied. Verification should also confirm lineage fields are preserved for task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id where applicable, without exposing raw Slack credentials or unrestricted tooling details.
items.rationale: Audit durability, searchability, and lineage are explicit requirements, and audit review is a named scenario in the baseline.
items.title: Verify durable, searchable audit evidence for governed outcomes

Optional fields should remain optional and not change the governed posture. actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can request channel context, summarize threads, prepare channel messages, prepare incident updates, and request channel announcements through governed Slack capabilities.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Can complete read actions directly. Can prepare drafts and announcement requests, but should stop at the approval boundary before any posting or execution step.
actor_expectations.summary_formalization: Business user who needs governed Slack channel and thread context, and who prepares messages or incident updates without directly posting to Slack.
actor_expectations.visibility_formalization: Can see bounded Slack channel context, thread summaries, message previews, incident update previews, and approval-request previews within allowed channels. Should not see raw Slack API or MCP operations, bulk export surfaces, or unrestricted workspace data.

actor_expectations.action_formalization: Can review approval-gated Slack posting requests and decide whether they may proceed, be revised, or be denied.
actor_expectations.approval_formalization: Owns the explicit approval boundary for posting-related actions described as approval-gated in the brief, but the exact scope by action type is still unclear.
actor_expectations.summary_formalization: Business reviewer who evaluates approval-gated Slack posting requests before any governed message is allowed to proceed toward posting.
actor_expectations.visibility_formalization: Can see the prepared message, announcement, or incident update preview plus enough channel context and audit evidence to make an approval decision within governed boundaries.

product_alignment.approval_posture_formalization: Read capabilities operate in a read-only posture. Communication-related capabilities operate in a prepare-only, approval-oriented posture that stops before direct mutation, with one-time or session-bound grants used for preview/request flows where supported.
product_alignment.governed_behavior_formalization: The product should return bounded Slack context for approved read scenarios, prepare communication previews or requests for write-adjacent scenarios, enforce channel and posting boundaries, produce audit evidence, and explicitly deny raw Slack API, MCP, bulk export, or unauthorized posting paths.

business_constraints.assistant_drafted_requirements_summary:
Expose governed Slack capabilities instead of raw Slack operations: The product shall present business-facing Slack capabilities for bounded channel context, thread inspection, message preparation, incident update preparation, and announcement request flows. It shall not expose raw Slack Web API operations, raw MCP operations, or direct workspace tool access to agents or end users.
Support bounded Slack context reads for channels and threads: The product shall allow governed retrieval of recent Slack channel context and thread context using bounded results only. Read flows shall support channel-scoped context lookup and thread summarization without enabling raw workspace export or unrestricted message retrieval.
Prepare Slack messages and incident updates without sending them: The product shall support preparation of channel messages and structured incident updates as preview-only outputs. These flows shall stop before posting, return draft or preview content for review, and avoid any direct Slack mutation during preparation.
Require approval before announcement or posting actions cross into execution: The product shall enforce an approval boundary for announcement-style posting and any other governed posting flow. Before execution, the system shall return an approval-ready request or preview and shall not post to Slack until the required approval condition is satisfied.
Enforce channel boundaries through allow-list policy: The product shall enforce channel allow-list rules on governed Slack reads, message preparation, incident update preparation, announcement requests, and any eventual posting action. Requests involving unauthorized channels shall be denied with a governed response rather than falling through to raw Slack access.
Record audit evidence for governed actions and policy denials: The product shall produce audit evidence for governed Slack reads, preview generation, approval requests, and denied actions. Audit records shall preserve enough traceability for reviewers to understand what capability was invoked, what boundary or policy applied, and why a request was allowed, stopped, or denied.
Explicitly deny raw export, raw tool access, and unauthorized posting: The product shall explicitly refuse requests for raw Slack API access, raw MCP access, bulk export, and posting to channels outside authorized policy. Denial behavior shall be intentional and user-visible, not an accidental backend failure or silent omission.
